lessons.title: Explain · Ads Manager and Opportunity Score

cards.h: 🗺️ The map of the full course
rows: ~16% of the exam. Campaign structure, the 6 key actions, Opportunity Score.
~9%. The 6 ODAX objectives and which objective to choose based on the business goal.
~13%. Auction vs Reservation, bid strategies, Learning Phase.
~17%. Pixel, CAPI (Conversions API), events and parameters.
~20%. Custom, Lookalike, Advantage+ Audience and catalogs.
~25%. The HEAVIEST area: creative, A/B Test, Brand Lift, Conversion Lift.

cards.label: HOW TO USE THIS MAP
cards.body: Notice the weights: Day 5 and Day 6 together make up ~45% of the exam. If you are short on time, prioritize mastering audiences and measurement over memorizing details from the first days.

Meta Ads Manager is the main tool where you manage all of your advertising on Facebook and Instagram. Many people think it only serves to design the ad, but it is actually the complete control center. From here you create the campaigns, yes, but you also configure how you are going to pay, define who you will show the ads to, connect your data sources, and review the results. Think of it like an airplane's cockpit: it doesn't just start the engine, it controls the entire flight. For example, La Esquina Café uses Ads Manager to launch its seasonal promo and, on the same screen, see how many reservations it generated.

cards.h: How this looks in Ads Manager (step by step)
cards.body: To get into the control center you follow this path: Meta Business Suite → left side menu → Ads Manager. Once inside, the top bar gives you access to the different sections: create campaigns with the green + Create button, see results in the columns and breakdowns tab, and from the nine-square menu (All tools) you reach audiences, billing, and data sources. The idea is that you don't need to leave Ads Manager to manage almost anything.

cards.h: The 6 key actions in Ads Manager
rows: Review how optimized your campaigns are

cards.body: Urban Store is an online fashion retailer that is going to run ads for its new collection. In Ads Manager they don't just design the ad: in the same place they connect the Pixel (a piece of code that tracks what people do on their website), register the card they will pay with, build the audience they will show the ads to, and afterward review the sales report. All from a single tool. It is a complete engine room, not an image editor.

The Opportunity Score is a rating from 0 to 100 that tells you how optimized your campaigns are. It works like this: Meta has a list of recommendations it has already tested and proven to improve results; the score measures how many of those recommendations you have already applied. If you are missing points, it isn't because your campaign is going to fail, but because there are pending suggestions you haven't turned on yet. Each missing point equals an unapplied recommendation. It is important to understand this clearly: it is a gauge of how much you followed best practices, not a crystal ball that predicts your sales.

cards.label: COMMON TRAP
cards.body: The Opportunity Score does NOT predict or guarantee real performance (it doesn't tell you your future ROAS). It only reflects the degree of adoption of proven recommendations.

cards.label: THREE MOMENTS
cards.body: It helps you before (from Account overview, before creating campaigns), during (as a campaign score when you create it — it doesn't affect the global one), and after (ongoing recommendations while it runs).

Sazón Kitchen (a food delivery restaurant) has a campaign that shows 68/100 on the Opportunity Score. That does not mean it will recover 68% of what it invests, or that its ROAS (return on ad spend) is 68%. It means there are proven recommendations it hasn't applied yet: for example, turning on Advantage+ audience (the automatic AI-powered audience) or adding the CAPI (Conversions API, a way to send data to Meta directly from your server). If Sazón Kitchen applies those suggestions, the score goes up; but the real sales results depend on the auction, not on the number.

cards.q: A coworker says: "Ads Manager is only for designing the creative." How do you respond?
options: They're right: it's only for the creative
It's incomplete: also billing, audiences, data
They're right: the creative is uploaded already made there
It's only true for Advantage+ campaigns
cards.explanation: Ads Manager is the control center: in addition to creating ads, it is used for opportunity score, reporting, audiences, billing, connecting data sources, and advertiser settings.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: The owner of Sazón Kitchen (delivery restaurant) sees that their campaign has an Opportunity Score of 64/100 and tells you: "So I'll only recover 64% of what I invest, right? Lower the budget." You have to correct the misunderstanding.

cards.q: What do you explain to the owner of Sazón Kitchen?
options: They're right: 64/100 projects the expected return
The score measures best practice adoption, not ROAS
You have to wait for 100/100 before investing
The score only applies to Advantage+ campaigns
cards.explanation: 64/100 = there are recommendations pending to apply. It is not a projection of return. The correct action is to review and apply those recommendations, not cut the budget over a misunderstanding.

cards.q: Where do I review those recommendations BEFORE creating more campaigns?
options: In Account overview, before the campaign
In the account's Brand Safety Center
cards.explanation: The 'before' moment of the opportunity score lives in Account overview: it helps you before creating campaigns. 'During' is when you create it, 'after' is while it runs.

cards.q: Which best describes the purpose of the Opportunity Score?
options: It predicts the ROAS the campaign will have
It shows which best practices are still to be applied
It measures creative quality vs the industry
It estimates next month's cost per result
cards.explanation: It is a 0-100 score + personalized recommendations. It does NOT predict performance — it reflects the adoption of proven best practices.

cards.q: The Opportunity Score helps you at three moments: before, ___ and after.
cards.explanation: Before (before creating), during (when you create it, without affecting the global one), and after (while it runs).

cards.q: When you create a campaign, the opportunity score that appears at the CAMPAIGN LEVEL:
options: Adds points to the global one when you apply recommendations
Does NOT affect the global one and only reflects that campaign
cards.explanation: The campaign-level score does not affect the account's global score. It reflects only that campaign. The global one only counts actions taken from Account overview.

cards.q: Connecting Pixel, CAPI, and datasets is done from Ads Manager in the ___ data sources action.
cards.explanation: Connect data sources: Pixel, CAPI, datasets. One of the 6 key actions of Ads Manager.

cards.q: The Opportunity Score goes from 60 to 78 after applying 3 recommendations. What can you affirm?
options: That the campaign's ROAS will rise about 30%
That you adopted more already-proven best practices
That the campaign's success is now guaranteed
That the ads' creative improved on its own
cards.explanation: The score measures the adoption of recommendations, not results. Raising it is good, but it doesn't guarantee performance.

cards.q: What is Ads Manager?
cards.a: Meta's control center: create campaigns, billing, audiences, reporting, connect data sources, advertiser settings. It's NOT only for creating ads.

cards.q: What does the Opportunity Score measure?
cards.a: A 0-100 score that shows how optimized your campaigns are based on proven recommendations you haven't applied yet. It does NOT predict real performance.

cards.q: Three moments when the Opportunity Score helps you
cards.a: Before (Account overview, before creating) · During (campaign score when you create it, doesn't affect the global one) · After (recommendations while it runs).

cards.q: Does the campaign-level score affect the global one?
cards.a: No. It reflects only that campaign. The global one only counts actions taken from Account overview.

cards.q: The 6 key actions in Ads Manager
cards.a: Opportunity score · Monitor reporting · Create audiences · Set up billing · Connect data sources · Advertiser settings.

lessons.title: Explain · Hierarchy Campaign → Ad set → Ad

cards.body: Every Meta campaign is built in a three-level structure nested together, like Russian dolls. The top level is the Campaign, which contains one or more ad sets, and each ad set contains one or more ads. The key thing is that at each level you decide different things and those decisions don't mix: what goes in the Campaign doesn't go in the Ad, and vice versa. Knowing which decision lives at each level is one of the most asked topics on the exam, so it's worth mastering. For example, for the flower shop Pétalos: a single sales campaign can have one ad set for young women and another for couples, and within each one, several different ads.

cards.h: What each level decides, broken down
cards.body: So you don't get confused, this is how the responsibility is split: 1. Campaign (the base): you decide the buying type (auction or reservation), the objective (what you want to achieve), and often the budget. 2. Ad set (the middle): you decide the audience (who you're talking to), the placements (which apps and sections it appears in), and the schedule. 3. Ad (the ad itself): you decide the creative (the visual material: image, video, text), the destination (where the click leads), and the tracking (tracking what people do).

cards.label: MENTAL RULE
cards.body: If the question is which objective, which buying type, how much to pay → Campaign. If it's who, where, when → Ad set. If it's how it looks, where it sends, what to measure → Ad.

cards.h: What you decide at each level
rows: Buying type · Objective · Budget · Bid · Special Ad Category · A/B test
Conversion (location, event, goal, dataset, attribution) · Budget & schedule · Audience · Placements · Dynamic creative
Partnership ad · Identity · Ad creative · Destination · Tracking · Languages

cards.label: THE BUDGET TRAP
cards.body: The Budget is set at the Campaign OR Ad set level, never both at the same time. At the Campaign level it is called Advantage+ campaign budget (formerly CBO) and is the default option.

FlowApp is a business management software sold to other companies (a B2B SaaS: software as a service, business to business). When building their campaign they decide, at the Campaign level: Auction as the buying type, a Sales objective, and the month's budget. At the Ad set level: the audience of operations teams, the placements where it will appear, and the conversion event that matters to them. At the Ad level: the video showing how the product works, the destination to the free-trial form, and tracking with the Pixel. One decision per level, without mixing.

cards.q: At which level do you make the selections for audience, conversion location, and placements?
cards.explanation: Audience, conversion settings, and placements are ad set level decisions. Campaign decides buying type/objective/budget; Ad decides creative/destination/tracking.

cards.q: Choosing the campaign Objective → ___ level.
cards.explanation: Objective = what you want to achieve = Campaign.

cards.q: Defining the placements → ___ level.
cards.explanation: Placements (where it appears) = Ad set.

cards.q: Choosing the image/video and the copy → ___ level.
cards.explanation: Creative = how it looks = Ad.

cards.q: Choosing Auction vs Reservation → ___ level.
cards.explanation: Buying type = Campaign, and it's the first decision.

cards.q: Buying type is a selection that can ONLY be made at the level:
cards.explanation: Buying type is the first decision and goes at the Campaign level (Auction or Reservation).

cards.q: The ad's destination (website, WhatsApp, form) and the tracking are configured at the level:
cards.explanation: Destination, creative, identity, and tracking are ad level decisions.

cards.q: The budget can be set at the Campaign AND Ad set level at the same time:
options: False: it goes in one of the two, never both
cards.explanation: The budget goes at ONE of the two levels. Default: Campaign (Advantage+ campaign budget).

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: You're setting up a Sales campaign for FlowApp (B2B SaaS). You have these pending decisions: (1) the audience of operations teams, (2) the product demo video, (3) Auction as the buying type. Place each one at its level.

cards.q: The audience of industrial companies is defined at the level:
cards.explanation: Audience = who = Ad set. Along with placements, schedule, and conversion settings.

cards.q: The installation video and Auction are decided, respectively, at:
cards.explanation: The creative (video) = Ad. The buying type (Auction) = Campaign, and it's the first decision.

cards.q: The 3 levels of a campaign in order
cards.a: Campaign → Ad set → Ad

cards.q: What is decided at the Campaign level?
cards.a: Buying type · Objective · Budget · Bid · Special Ad Category · A/B test

cards.q: What is decided at the Ad set level?
cards.a: Conversion (location, event, goal, dataset, attribution) · Budget & schedule · Audience · Placements · Dynamic creative

cards.q: What is decided at the Ad level?
cards.a: Partnership ad · Identity · Ad creative · Destination · Tracking · Languages

cards.q: The budget trap
cards.a: It's set at the Campaign OR Ad set level, never both. Default: Campaign = Advantage+ campaign budget (formerly CBO).

cards.body: Advantage+ is Meta's set of tools that use AI (artificial intelligence) and automation (the machine makes decisions on its own, without you doing them by hand) to connect your ads with the people most likely to buy or act. The underlying idea is simple: instead of you guessing who to show the ad to or how much to pay, the AI analyzes signals in real time (while the campaign runs) and adjusts on the fly. You set the general direction and the objective; Advantage+ takes care of fine-tuning the execution to deliver the most effective ads to the right people.

DentalCare is a dental clinic that wants to sell whitening treatments. Instead of the owner guessing who is interested (young people? couples getting married?), Advantage+ lets the AI explore real behavioral signals (who searches for dental topics, who engages with similar ads) and find those prospects while the campaign is running. DentalCare defines the goal (more whitening appointments) and the AI optimizes who to show the ads to and when.

rows: Automate the ENTIRE campaign: Advantage+ Sales, App, Leads
Automate ONE aspect: Audience, Placement, Budget, Creative, Destination

cards.body: Complete campaign solutions are the ones that let the AI handle the entire campaign from start to finish, not just one small step. There are exactly three and it's worth memorizing them: 1. Advantage+ Sales (to generate sales, for example in an online store), 2. Advantage+ App (to get installs and actions inside a mobile app), and 3. Advantage+ Leads (to capture prospects via forms). For example, the online store Aceros del Bajío would use Advantage+ Sales and let the AI build the audience, placements, and budget on its own. Later you'll see the single-step ones, which automate just one part.

rows: Advantage+ audience, custom, lookalike, detailed targeting

cards.label: COMMON TRAP
cards.body: Don't confuse Advantage+ campaigns (complete: Sales/App/Leads) with Advantage+ tools (single-step: Audience, Placement, Budget, Creative, Destination). The exam mixes both in the distractors.

cards.q: Which best describes Meta Advantage+?
options: A graphic design tool for ads
An AI suite that matches ads and audiences
A payment method for large campaigns
cards.explanation: Advantage+ = AI + automation in real time to match ads with whoever is most likely to act. It is not design, reporting, or billing.

cards.q: What is Meta Advantage+?
cards.a: A suite of AI + automation in real time to match ads with the people most likely to act. It optimizes by real-time data, effectiveness, and relevance.

cards.q: The 2 categories of Advantage+
cards.a: Complete campaign solutions (the entire campaign: Sales, App, Leads) · Single-step (one aspect: Audience, Placement, Budget, Creative, Destination).

cards.q: The 3 Advantage+ complete campaign solutions
cards.a: Advantage+ Sales · Advantage+ App · Advantage+ Leads

cards.q: What does Advantage+ campaign budget do?
cards.a: It dynamically allocates the budget to the best-performing ad set/ad. The default single-step budget solution. (Formerly CBO.)

cards.q: What do Advantage+ catalog ads do?
cards.a: They show products from a catalog to whoever is most likely to find them relevant. Available when you choose Sales.

cards.q: A client wants to automate their ENTIRE campaign using AI for website sales. What do you recommend?
cards.explanation: Automating the ENTIRE campaign = complete campaign solution = Advantage+ Sales. The others are single-step.

cards.q: Which of these are single-step Advantage+ solutions? Select 2.
cards.explanation: Placements and audience are single-step. Sales and Leads campaigns are complete campaign solutions.

cards.q: Which of these is NOT an Advantage+ complete campaign solution?
cards.explanation: The 3 complete ones are Sales, App, and Leads. Advantage+ Catalog Ads is single-step (showing products from a catalog).

cards.q: If a question says 'CBO', what does it refer to?
cards.explanation: CBO = Campaign Budget Optimization, today renamed to Advantage+ campaign budget.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: Two clients, two levels of delegation
cards.body: Client A wants the AI to manage their entire sales campaign. Client B wants to control the campaign himself but have the AI only choose the audience for him. What do you give each one?

cards.q: Client A (automate the entire sales campaign):
cards.explanation: The entire sales campaign = complete campaign = Advantage+ Sales.

cards.q: Client B (automate only the audience):
cards.explanation: Automating only one aspect = single-step. Audience = Advantage+ audience.

cards.body: Meta publishes a framework of 7 Performance Best Practices, which are the official recommendations for a campaign to work well. Don't see them as a loose list to memorize by heart: each one solves a specific problem in campaign building. If you understand what each one solves, you'll answer them on the exam even if they change the wording. The first 6 are things you do before or during the campaign; the seventh happens after. In this lesson you'll see the first 4, and in the next one the last 3.

The first practice, Automate campaign setup, says to leverage the AI tools of Advantage+ instead of configuring everything by hand. The idea is that the machine makes better decisions about structure, audience, and budget than what you'd build manually, because it processes signals in real time. You define the goal and let automation execute. This also reduces human error and saves you hours of setup. Example: the dental clinic DentalCare, instead of building 8 ad sets one by one, lets Advantage+ Sales (the automated sales campaign) structure everything at once.

The second practice, Align objectives & goals, is the easiest to fail. The objective is what you tell Meta to optimize for (sales, traffic, awareness), and the goal is what you actually want to achieve in real life. If they don't match, Meta optimizes the wrong metric and you spend money without real results. For example, if you ask for traffic, Meta brings you cheap clicks even if no one buys. Example: the real estate agency Casa Plus wants to close quotes, so its objective should be Sales or Leads, never Awareness.

The third practice, Consider your audience, asks you to combine what you already know about your customers with what the AI can discover on its own. Instead of locking the campaign into a fixed, small audience, you give Meta your data as a starting point and let Advantage+ audience (the automatic audience) explore similar profiles you wouldn't have found. That way you don't just compete for the obvious people and you open up reach to new but relevant prospects. Example: the online store Aceros del Bajío uploads its list of best customers and the AI expands toward people with similar behavior.

The fourth practice, Optimize creative & placements, has two parts. The creative (the ad's material: image, video, text) should be varied: different messages, formats, and visuals so Meta tests which works best with each person. The placements (where the ad appears, like Feed, Stories, or Reels) are best left on Advantage+ placements (automatic placements) so the machine distributes the ad where it performs best. A single static ad in a single place limits results. Example: the flower shop Pétalos uploads several videos and images of its arrangements and lets them run across all placements, not one fixed banner.

cards.q: FitZone (gym) optimizes for 'page visits' but the real goal is to close memberships. Which Best Practice is failing?
cards.explanation: BP 2 — Align objectives & goals: the campaign objective must align with the business goal. Optimizing for visits when you want sales is the classic failure.

cards.body: The fifth practice, Focus on signals, says to make decisions with high-quality data instead of hunches. Signals are the information your business sends Meta about what people do: the Pixel (code on your website that records visits, purchases, and other actions), the CAPI (Conversions API, a connection that sends those same data directly from your server, more reliable than the Pixel alone), the datasets (where those actions are stored), and the events (like "added to cart" or "purchased"). The more and better signals you give Meta, the better the AI optimizes. Example: the online store Aceros del Bajío, with 3 months of Pixel plus CAPI running, gives Meta solid data to find real buyers.

cards.h: BP 6 — Get the most out of budget & bidding
cards.body: The sixth practice, Get the most out of budget & bidding, asks you to let the AI allocate the money instead of doing it by hand. The budget is how much you invest, and the bidding is how much your campaign is willing to pay for each result in the auction. With Advantage+ campaign budget (the automatic campaign-level budget, formerly called CBO) Meta moves the money toward the ad set delivering the best results, in real time. Splitting the budget by hand usually leaves money stuck in ads that don't work. Example: at the real estate agency Casa Plus, the budget flows on its own toward the ad set closing the most quotes.

The seventh practice, Validate results, consists of measuring the real impact of the campaign to learn and improve the next ones. This is where tools like A/B tests (showing two versions to similar audiences and comparing which wins) and lift studies (incrementality studies: they compare those who saw the ad against those who didn't, to know how much extra you sold thanks to advertising) come in. The key thing for the exam: this practice happens post-campaign (after the campaign ran), which is why it does not appear in the Opportunity Score recommendations, which only cover what you can apply before or during. Example: the real estate agency Casa Plus, at the end of the month, runs an A/B test to decide which video to use the next quarter.

cards.label: PRO TIP
cards.body: If a question mentions "opportunity score recommendations," it's about BP 1-6. BP 7 (Validate results) isn't measured there because it happens post-campaign.

cards.q: Which Best Practice focuses on informing decisions with high-quality data?
options: BP 6 — Get the most out of budget & bidding
cards.explanation: BP 5 — Focus on signals: "Inform decision making and optimize your strategy with high-quality data." Signals = Pixel, CAPI, datasets, events.

lessons.title: Exercise your memory · order the 7 BPs
lessons.instruction: Meta's 7 BPs go in a specific order. Reorder with ▲▼ and verify.
items: Get the most out of budget & bidding
lessons.explanation: The Opportunity Score recommendations correspond to BP 1-6. BP 7 (Validate results) happens post-campaign and isn't measured in the opportunity score.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: The campaign spends but doesn't sell
cards.body: Casa Plus (real estate agency) has spent 2 weeks with a campaign that gathers many clicks but zero quotes. You check and the configured objective is Traffic, even though the client wants sales. Plus there's only 1 static ad and the Pixel isn't connected.

cards.q: The main failure of a misaligned objective corresponds to:
cards.explanation: Objective (Traffic) ≠ business goal (sales) → BP 2 — Align objectives & goals.

cards.q: The Pixel not being connected breaches above all:
cards.explanation: Without the Pixel there are no quality signals to optimize conversions → BP 5 — Focus on signals.

cards.q: Which is NOT one of the 7 official Performance Best Practices?
cards.explanation: Frequency caps is NOT on the list. The 7 are: Automate · Align · Audience · Creative & placements · Signals · Budget & bidding · Validate.

cards.q: Why is BP 7 (Validate results) NOT in the Opportunity Score recommendations?
options: Because Meta deprecated it several years ago
Because it only applies to Advantage+ campaigns
Because it happens post-campaign, not before
cards.explanation: The Opportunity Score evaluates recommendations applicable before or during (BP 1-6). Validate results happens after the campaign ended.

cards.q: BP ___ is called Focus on signals and uses high-quality data to inform decisions.
cards.explanation: BP 5 — Focus on signals. Signals = Pixel, CAPI, datasets, events.

cards.q: The Opportunity Score recommendations correspond to BP 1 through ___.
cards.explanation: BP 1-6. BP 7 (Validate results) happens post-campaign.

cards.q: What are the 7 Performance Best Practices?
cards.a: 1. Automate setup · 2. Align objectives & goals · 3. Consider audience · 4. Optimize creative & placements · 5. Focus on signals · 6. Get the most out of budget & bidding · 7. Validate results

cards.q: Which BPs correspond to the Opportunity Score?
cards.a: BP 1-6. BP 7 (Validate results) happens post-campaign.

cards.q: In one sentence, what does BP 2 solve?
cards.a: That the campaign objective matches the business goal (not optimizing the wrong metric, e.g. traffic when you want sales).

cards.q: What does BP 5 — Focus on signals solve?
cards.a: Informing/optimizing decisions with high-quality data: Pixel, CAPI, datasets, events.

cards.body: Meta Business Suite is the unified hub from which you manage your entire business across Meta's apps: Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp, all in one place. While Ads Manager is specific to campaigns, Business Suite is the level above: here you manage portfolios, organic content, messages, billing, brand safety, and your team's permissions. Think of it as the business's headquarters, not just the ad workshop. For example, the dealership AutoMax answers Instagram messages, reviews invoices, and grants access to its agency without leaving Business Suite.

A business portfolio (formerly called Business Manager) is a container that groups all of a brand's assets in one place: the Facebook Page, the Instagram account, the ad account (where the ads run), the product catalog, etc. The advantage of having them together is huge: you manage everything in a unified way, control who can touch what, collaborate with external agencies without sharing passwords, and receive notifications on a single dashboard. Without a portfolio, each asset would live on its own and you'd grant access by lending your personal login. Example: the dealership AutoMax puts its Page, its Instagram, its ad account, and its car catalog into a single portfolio.

Let's continue with AutoMax (the dealership). Before having a portfolio, they gave their agency the password to the owner's Facebook: a huge risk. With a business portfolio, instead, they add the agency as a controlled access and give it permission only over the ad account, not over the whole operation. If tomorrow they stop working with that agency, they remove the access with one click and no one has to change passwords. That's what the portfolio solves: fine-grained control over who enters what, without exposing your personal accounts.

cards.h: How this looks in Business Suite (step by step)
cards.body: To manage access and assets you follow this path: Meta Business Suite → Settings gear at the bottom left → Business settings. There, in the side menu, you find Users divided into People, Partners, and System users; further down is Accounts for your Pages, ad accounts, and catalogs. To invite someone you choose People → Add, enter their email, and assign the permission level. All the control over who does what lives here, not inside Ads Manager.

cards.h: Types of users in a portfolio
rows: Individuals with partial access or full control
Other businesses (they require their own portfolio + business ID)
Servers or software with API access (not all accounts have it)
See who has full control · work only on assigned assets
Set up CAPI · monitor events · edit applications · create access tokens
Manage everything: settings, people, tools, assets · delete the portfolio
View or manage financial info (transactions, invoices)
Basic access for min 3 days, max 75 days

cards.label: SUBTLE DIFFERENCE
cards.body: A user can have full control of ONE account without having full control of the entire portfolio. This allows fine-grained permissions without losing oversight.

cards.q: Main difference between People, Partners, and System users?
options: They only differ in the billing discount
People, other businesses, and servers with API
cards.explanation: People = individual people. Partners = other businesses (their portfolio + business ID). System users = servers/software with API access.

cards.q: An advertiser wants a surface to manage billing, brand safety, and permissions across several accounts. Which one?
cards.explanation: Meta Business Suite is the unified hub. Ads Manager is for creating/managing campaigns.

cards.q: To collaborate with an agency that has its own portfolio, you add it as:
cards.explanation: Agencies are added as Partners (they have their own portfolio + business ID).

cards.q: 'Advanced — Finance' allows:
options: Viewing and managing transactions and invoices
Accessing system users and external APIs
cards.explanation: Advanced — Finance is a specific permission for financial matters. Separate from Full control.

cards.q: ___ users are servers or software with API access.
cards.explanation: System users = servers/software/APIs.

cards.q: Temporary access lasts a minimum of 3 days and a maximum of ___ days.
cards.explanation: Maximum 75 days. A literal exam question.

cards.q: To set up the CAPI you need Partial access (___).
cards.explanation: Partial access (apps): set up CAPI, monitor events, edit applications, create access tokens.

cards.q: What is Meta Business Suite?
cards.a: A centralized surface to manage marketing, ads, and client accounts on Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp.

cards.q: What is a Business Portfolio?
cards.a: A grouper of assets (FB Page, IG, ad account, catalog) in one place, with unified management of assets and access.

cards.q: The 3 types of users in a portfolio
cards.a: People (individuals) · Partners (other businesses with their portfolio + business ID) · System users (servers/software with API access).

cards.q: Which access allows setting up the CAPI?
cards.a: Partial access (apps): set up CAPI, monitor events, edit applications, create access tokens.

cards.q: Temporary access in a portfolio: how long does it last?
cards.a: Minimum 3 days, maximum 75 days.

cards.q: Partial access vs Full control
cards.a: Partial: only assigned assets, see who has full control. Full: manage everything (settings, people, tools, assets) + delete the portfolio.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: You need to grant access: (1) an agency that has its own portfolio and will set up the CAPI, (2) the accountant who only views invoices, (3) a freelancer who helps for 2 weeks. Assign the right one to each.

cards.q: The agency (its own portfolio + set up CAPI):
cards.explanation: Agency with its own portfolio = Partner. To set up CAPI it needs Partial access (apps).

cards.q: The accountant (invoices only) and the freelancer (2 weeks):
options: Full control of the portfolio for both
Finance for the accountant, Temporary for the freelancer
System user for the accountant, Partner for the freelancer
cards.explanation: Accountant → Advanced — Finance (invoices/transactions). Temporary freelancer → Advanced — Temporary (3-75 days).

cards.body: Billing and payments is the Business Suite section where you handle everything related to your ad money: what you pay with, how much you've spent, and where you download your invoices. You can add your own payment method (such as a card) or share one that lives in another portfolio. What matters for the exam is access control: changing the payment method can't be done by just anyone, you need admin access or the finance (view and manage) role. Basic or temporary access isn't enough. Example: at the restaurant Sazón Kitchen, the waiter with basic access can view reports, but only the owner with the admin role changes the card.

rows: Add methods and connect credit lines
View available credit · apply for monthly invoicing
Billing details and payment responsibilities with partners

cards.body: The accountant at Hotel Lumière has a narrow task: close the month's bookkeeping. They go into Invoices to download the receipts and into Payment activity to review how much was spent. What they can't do with their access is change the card the hotel pays with: for that they'd need the admin or finance (view and manage) role. This example shows the logic of permissions: you grant exactly what's needed for the task, no more, no less. That way the accountant does their job without being able to touch the advertising operation.

The Brand Safety & Suitability Center is the section where you decide next to what type of content your ads appear, so your brand doesn't show up alongside something that doesn't suit it. It offers two things: controls to include or exclude certain content or certain publishers (the sites, pages, or creators where your ad is shown), and transparency tools that show you publisher information and where your ads were delivered. These controls don't live only here: you can also apply them at the ad set → placements level in Ads Manager. Example: the pet store PetCare uses these controls so its ads don't appear next to controversial content.

rows: Type of content: Expanded (default), Moderate, Limited — affects reach
Lists YOU create to avoid certain publishers
Gaming, News, Politics, Religion (only FB in-stream/reels)

cards.label: PRO TIP
cards.body: To exclude sensitive content in the simplest way, use the inventory filters (Moderate or Limited). Block lists require building the list; topic exclusions only work on very specific placements.

cards.q: Simplest way to exclude groups of sensitive content in the Brand Safety Center?
options: Exclude topics such as Gaming, News, Politics
Turn on content type exclusions for live video
cards.explanation: Inventory filters (Expanded/Moderate/Limited) are the simplest control with the broadest reach. Block lists = manual work; topic exclusions only FB in-stream/reels.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: The owner of PetCare (pet products) doesn't want their ads to appear next to controversial content, and doesn't have time to build publisher-by-publisher lists. Plus the accountant needs to be able to download the month's invoices.

cards.q: To avoid sensitive content without building lists by hand, the simplest route is:
options: Raise the inventory filter to Moderate or Limited
cards.explanation: Inventory filters (Moderate/Limited) = simplest control with the broadest reach, without building lists manually. Topic exclusions do NOT cover all placements (only FB in-stream/reels).

cards.q: For the accountant to download invoices, what do they need?
options: Full control of the entire portfolio
A finance role with access to Invoices
To be a System user of the portfolio
cards.explanation: Invoices lives in Billing & payments. For financial matters you use the finance role / Advanced — Finance, not Full control or a system user.

cards.q: Inventory filters has 3 levels. Which gives the MOST reach?
cards.explanation: Expanded (default) = most reach. Moderate = balance. Limited = less reach, more exclusion. Strict doesn't exist.

cards.q: Topic exclusions (Gaming, News, Politics, Religion) are ONLY in:
cards.explanation: Topic exclusions apply exclusively to Facebook in-stream and reels.

cards.q: What access is needed to change the payment method?
options: Being a System user is enough
cards.explanation: Changing payment methods requires admin access or the finance role (view and manage).

cards.q: To exclude sensitive content in the simplest way, use the ___.
cards.explanation: Inventory filters (Expanded/Moderate/Limited): the simplest control with the broadest reach.

cards.q: The Brand Safety Center is accessible ONLY from Business Suite:
options: False: also at the ad set → placements level in Ads Manager
cards.explanation: The Brand Safety controls are also at the ad set → placements level in Ads Manager.

cards.q: What access is needed to change the payment method?
cards.a: Admin access or finance role (view and manage). Basic/temporary access isn't enough.

cards.q: Options within Billing & payments
cards.a: Accounts · Payment activity · Invoices · Payment methods · Credit lines · Legal entities.

cards.q: The 4 types of controls in the Brand Safety Center
cards.a: Inventory filters (Expanded/Moderate/Limited) · Publisher block lists · Topic exclusions (Gaming/News/Politics/Religion) · Content type exclusions.

cards.q: Inventory filters: levels and which gives the most reach
cards.a: Expanded (default, most reach) · Moderate · Limited (less reach, more exclusion). It's the simplest control.

cards.q: Where do topic exclusions work?
cards.a: Only on Facebook in-stream and reels. That's why inventory filters are more useful for general exclusion.

cards.label: REAL SCENARIO
cards.h: B2B client with a serious budget
cards.body: Industrial Suite is a B2B Shopify store that sells office equipment to large companies (>500 employees). Its budget is $30,000 USD/month. They have had the Meta Pixel installed for 3 months and receive ~800 Purchase events per month. The main goal: generate sales on their website. The client asks you: which objective, which type of Advantage+, and which Best Practice should I prioritize?

cards.q: Which Campaign Objective do you recommend for Industrial Suite?
options: Awareness — so they get to know the brand
Sales — because the direct goal is to generate sales on the site
Engagement — so they interact with the posts
cards.explanation: The client explicitly said they want sales on their website. The correct objective is Sales. Awareness/Engagement don't optimize for conversion, and Leads would be valid if it were about capturing forms (which it isn't).

cards.q: Given that the client wants to automate the ENTIRE campaign, which Advantage+ solution do you apply?
cards.explanation: To automate the entire campaign with a Sales objective → Advantage+ Sales campaigns. App is for mobile apps, Leads is for forms, and Advantage+ creative is only single-step (it automates just the creative, not the whole campaign).

cards.q: The client already has a Pixel with 3 months of data. Which Best Practice do you prioritize to get more out of that information?
cards.explanation: BP 5 — Focus on signals: "Inform decision making with high-quality data." Having 3 months of Pixel with Purchase events is exactly the quality signal this BP aims to leverage. Ideally also add Conversions API (CAPI) to complement the Pixel.

lessons.title: Match key concepts from Day 1
lessons.instruction: Tap the term and then its definition. Each mistake costs 1 ❤️.
pairs: 0-100 optimization score based on experimentally proven recommendations
Buying type, objective, budget, bid, A/B test
Complete campaign solution that automates an entire sales campaign
Single-step solution that automates audience selection
Dynamically allocates budget to the best-performing ad set (formerly CBO)
Making decisions with high-quality data: Pixel, CAPI, datasets
Grouper of assets (Pages, IG, ad accounts, catalogs) with centralized permissions
Other businesses with their own portfolio + business ID
Simple Brand Safety Center control: Expanded, Moderate, Limited
Min 3 days, max 75 days in a business portfolio

cards.q: The ___ is a 0-100 score that shows how optimized your campaigns are based on experimentally proven recommendations.
cards.explanation: The Opportunity Score measures the adoption of recommendations, NOT real performance.

cards.q: Audience, placements, and schedule decisions are made at the ___ level.
cards.explanation: Ad set = who, where, when. Campaign = which objective and how much. Ad = how it looks.

cards.q: The default budget optimization at the campaign level is called Advantage+ ___ budget.
cards.explanation: Advantage+ campaign budget (formerly CBO) dynamically distributes the budget across ad sets.

cards.q: The 3 Advantage+ complete campaign solutions are Sales, App, and ___.
cards.explanation: The 3 complete campaigns are Advantage+ Sales · App · Leads. The others are objectives, not complete Advantage+ campaigns.

cards.q: BP ___ is called Focus on signals and refers to using high-quality data to inform decisions.
cards.explanation: BP 5 — Focus on signals. The signals = Pixel, CAPI, datasets, events.

cards.q: To exclude groups of sensitive content in the simplest way, use the ___.
cards.explanation: Inventory filters (Expanded/Moderate/Limited) are the simplest control with the broadest reach. Block lists require building the list manually.

cards.q: ___ users are servers or software with API access in a business portfolio.
cards.explanation: System users = servers/software/APIs. Partner = another business. People = individual people.

cards.q: Temporary access in a portfolio lasts a minimum of 3 days and a maximum of ___ days.
cards.explanation: Maximum 75 days. It's a literal exam question — memorize it.

cards.q: To change the payment method you need admin access or the ___ role.
cards.explanation: Changing payment methods requires admin access or the finance (view and manage) role.

cards.q: The Best Practice that happens post-campaign and does NOT enter the Opportunity Score is BP ___.
cards.explanation: BP 7 — Validate results happens after the campaign; the Opportunity Score only covers BP 1-6.

cards.q: An advertiser wants a surface to manage billing, brand safety, and permissions across multiple accounts. Which do you recommend?
cards.explanation: Meta Business Suite is the unified hub for managing portfolios, billing, brand safety, and permissions. Ads Manager is for creating/managing campaigns, not for managing the business.

cards.q: Buying type is a selection that can ONLY be made at the level:
cards.explanation: Buying type is the first decision and goes at the Campaign level (Auction or Reservation). Ad set and Ad don't allow changing it.

cards.q: Which of these is NOT an Advantage+ complete campaign solution?
cards.explanation: The 3 complete campaign solutions are Sales, App, and Leads. Advantage+ Catalog Ads is a feature within Sales (single-step for showing products from a catalog).

cards.q: To collaborate with an agency that has its own portfolio, how do you add it to your portfolio?
cards.explanation: Agencies are added as Partners because they have their own business portfolio + business ID. People are individuals; System users are servers/APIs.

cards.q: If an exam question says 'CBO', what does it refer to?
cards.explanation: CBO = Campaign Budget Optimization, today renamed to Advantage+ campaign budget. It dynamically allocates budget to the best-performing ad set.

cards.q: Why is BP 7 (Validate results) NOT in the Opportunity Score recommendations?
options: Because Meta deprecated it several years ago
Because it only applies to Advantage+ campaigns
Because it happens post-campaign, not before
cards.explanation: The Opportunity Score evaluates recommendations applicable before or during the campaign (BP 1-6). BP 7 — Validate results happens after the campaign ended.

cards.q: The 'Advanced — Finance' permission level allows:
options: Viewing and managing transactions and invoices
Accessing system users and external APIs
cards.explanation: Advanced — Finance is a specific permission for financial matters (invoices, transactions, payment methods). It is separate from Full control.

cards.q: Inventory filters has 3 levels. Which gives the MOST reach?
cards.explanation: Expanded (default) = most reach, least filtering. Moderate = balance. Limited = less reach, more exclusion of sensitive content. Strict doesn't exist.

cards.q: Topic exclusions (Gaming, News, Politics, Religion) are available ONLY in:
cards.explanation: Topic exclusions apply exclusively to Facebook in-stream and reels. They don't work in other placements — that's why inventory filters are more useful for general exclusion.

cards.q: Which Advantage+ single-step solution was formerly called CBO?
cards.explanation: CBO (Campaign Budget Optimization) → Advantage+ campaign budget. It's the most-mentioned rebranding on the exam.

cards.q: When you create a campaign, the opportunity score that appears at the CAMPAIGN LEVEL:
options: Adds points to the global one when you apply recommendations
Does NOT affect the global one and only reflects that campaign
cards.explanation: The campaign-level score does not affect the account's global score. It reflects only that specific campaign. The global score only counts actions taken from Account overview.

cards.q: Which of the following are within the Billing and payments section in Business Suite? Select 3.
options: Credit lines (available credit and monthly invoicing)
cards.explanation: Billing and payments includes: Accounts, Payment activity, Invoices, Payment methods, Credit lines, Legal entities. Audience Insights is a separate analytics tool, not part of billing.

questions.q: Does the Opportunity Score predict the campaign's ROAS?
options: No, it only measures the adoption of recommendations
questions.explanation: The Opportunity Score does NOT predict performance — it measures the adoption of proven recommendations.

questions.q: Can the budget be set at the Campaign AND Ad set level simultaneously?
options: Yes, in both at the same time
No, only in one of the two
questions.explanation: The budget goes at ONE of the two levels, never both. Default: Campaign level (Advantage+ campaign budget).

questions.q: Is Advantage+ Sales a single-step solution?
options: No, it's a complete campaign (it automates everything)
questions.explanation: Advantage+ Sales is a complete campaign. The single-step ones are: audience, placements, budget, creative, destination.

questions.q: Do the 7 Performance Best Practices include 'Maximize frequency caps'?
questions.explanation: Frequency caps is NOT a BP. The 7 BPs are: Automate · Align · Audience · Creative & placements · Signals · Budget & bidding · Validate.

questions.q: Does temporary access in a portfolio last a maximum of 90 days?
questions.explanation: Minimum 3 days, maximum 75 days. Memorize it literally.

questions.q: Do Partners in a portfolio need to have their own business ID?
options: Yes, they require their own portfolio + ID
questions.explanation: Partners are OTHER businesses. That's why they need their own separate portfolio + business ID.

questions.q: Does the 'Limited' inventory filter give LESS reach than 'Expanded'?
questions.explanation: Limited = more exclusion = less reach. Expanded (default) = less filtering = more reach.

questions.q: Is the Brand Safety Center accessible ONLY from Business Suite?
options: No, also at the ad set level in Ads Manager (placements)
questions.explanation: The Brand Safety controls are also available at the ad set → placements level in Ads Manager.

questions.q: Do topic exclusions work in Audience Network?
options: No, only FB in-stream and reels
questions.explanation: Topic exclusions apply exclusively to Facebook in-stream and reels.

questions.q: If you pass the diagnostic with ≥80%, can you skip the day's lessons?
options: Yes, jump straight to the Unit Quiz
No, you must always do the lessons
questions.explanation: The diagnostic is the shortcut: if you master the content, ≥80% lets you skip straight to the Unit Quiz.

cards.q: What is Ads Manager?
cards.a: Meta's control center: create campaigns, configure billing, manage audiences, view reporting, connect data sources, adjust advertiser settings. It's NOT only for creating ads.

cards.q: What does the Opportunity Score measure?
cards.a: A 0-100 score that shows how optimized your campaigns are based on experimentally proven recommendations you haven't applied yet. It does NOT predict real performance.

cards.q: Three moments when the Opportunity Score helps you
cards.a: Before (Account overview, before creating campaigns) · During (campaign score when you create it, doesn't affect the global one) · After (ongoing recommendations while it runs).

cards.q: The 3 levels of a campaign in order
cards.a: Campaign → Ad set → Ad

cards.q: What is decided at the Campaign level?
cards.a: Buying type · Objective · Budget · Bid · Special Ad Category · A/B test

cards.q: What is decided at the Ad set level?
cards.a: Conversion (location, event, goal, dataset, attribution) · Budget & schedule · Audience · Placements · Dynamic creative

cards.q: What is decided at the Ad level?
cards.a: Partnership ad · Identity · Ad setup · Ad creative · Destination · Tracking · Languages

cards.q: What is Meta Advantage+?
cards.a: A suite of solutions that uses AI + automation in real time to match ads with the people most likely to act. It optimizes by real-time data, effectiveness, and relevance.

cards.q: The 2 categories of Advantage+
cards.a: Complete campaign solutions (they automate the entire campaign: Sales, App, Leads) · Single-step solutions (they automate one aspect: Audience, Placement, Budget, Creative, Destination).

cards.q: The 3 Advantage+ complete campaign solutions
cards.a: Advantage+ Sales · Advantage+ App · Advantage+ Leads

cards.q: What does Advantage+ campaign budget do?
cards.a: It dynamically allocates the budget to the best-performing ad set/ad. It's the default single-step solution for budget automation. (It was formerly called CBO.)

cards.q: What do Advantage+ catalog ads do?
cards.a: They show products from a catalog to the people most likely to find them relevant. Available when you choose Sales as the objective.

cards.q: What are the 7 Performance Best Practices?
cards.a: 1. Automate setup · 2. Align objectives & goals · 3. Consider audience · 4. Optimize creative & placements · 5. Focus on signals · 6. Get the most out of budget & bidding · 7. Validate results

cards.q: Which BPs correspond to the Opportunity Score recommendations?
cards.a: BP 1-6. BP 7 (Validate results) happens post-campaign.

cards.q: In one sentence, what does BP 2 — Align objectives & goals solve?
cards.a: That the campaign objective matches the real business goal (not optimizing the wrong metric, e.g. traffic when you want sales).

cards.q: What is Meta Business Suite?
cards.a: A centralized surface to manage marketing, ads, and client accounts on Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp.

cards.q: What is a Business Portfolio?
cards.a: A grouper of assets (Facebook Page, Instagram, ad account, catalog, etc.) in one place, with unified management of assets and access.

cards.q: The 3 types of users in a portfolio
cards.a: People (individuals) · Partners (other businesses with their own portfolio + business ID) · System users (servers/software with API access).

cards.q: Difference: Partial access vs Full control in a portfolio
cards.a: Partial: work only on assigned assets, see who has full control. Full: manage everything (settings, people, tools, assets, claim/remove) + delete the portfolio.

cards.q: Temporary access in a portfolio: how much time?
cards.a: Minimum 3 days, maximum 75 days.

cards.q: Which access allows setting up the Conversions API (CAPI)?
cards.a: Partial access (apps): set up CAPI, monitor events, edit applications, and create access tokens.

cards.q: What access is needed to change the payment method?
cards.a: Admin access or finance role (view and manage). Basic/temporary access isn't enough.

cards.q: The 4 types of controls in the Brand Safety Center
cards.a: Inventory filters (Expanded/Moderate/Limited) · Publisher block lists · Topic exclusions (Gaming/News/Politics/Religion) · Content type exclusions (live video, non-partner).

cards.q: Inventory filters (levels)
cards.a: Expanded, Moderate, or Limited. It's the simplest control with the broadest coverage to exclude sensitive content in the Brand Safety and Suitability Center.

cards.q: Advantage+ complete campaign solutions
cards.a: Advantage+ sales, app, and leads campaigns: the AI automates the ENTIRE campaign. Single-step (a single aspect): audience, placements, campaign budget, creative, destination, catalog ads.

cards.q: Employee access vs admin access (Business Manager)
cards.a: Employee only accesses the assigned assets. Adding/removing people, creating accounts/Pages, or deleting the business require admin access.

cards.q: Creation levels and what's decided at each one
cards.a: Campaign: buying type, objective, budget, bid. Ad set: conversion/performance goal, budget/schedule, audience, placements. Ad: creative, destination, tracking.
